coredata.dc:title: Experimental and theoretical insights into colossal supercapacitive performance of graphene quantum dots incorporated Ni3S2/CoS2/MoS2 electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Sangabathula, Omkar
Kandasamy, Manikandan
Chakraborty, Brahmananda
Sharma, Chandra S.
coredata.dc:description: Transition metal sulfides (TMS) are promising electrode materials for supercapacitor applications due to their unmatchable advantages. To overcome the deficiency of individual metal sulfides, the combination of one or more pseudocapacitive transition metals with conductive carbon material can be a plausible solution. In this work, a simple and one-step hydrothermal synthesis method is used to fabricate the flower-like Ni3S2/CoS2/MoS2 (NCMS) and Ni3S2/CoS2/MoS2-GQDs (NCMS-G) directly on Ni foam and used it as a binder-free electrode for high-performance supercapacitor applications. The NCMS-G-2.5 electrode possessed colossal specific capacitance (2364 Fg−1 at the current density of 2 Ag−1), excellent rate capability (55 % at the current density of 10 Ag−1), as well as good cycling stability (93 % retention after 10,000 cycles at the current density of 20 Ag−1). Furthermore, assembled NCMS-G-2.5//rGO asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device delivered an excellent energy density of 84.8 Wh kg−1 and superior cycling performance (98 % capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles). We have investigated the interactions between Ni3S2/CoS2/MoS2 and carbon based nanostructure GQDs employing state-of-the-art Density Functional Theory (DFT) simulations. The interactions between Ni3S2/CoS2/MoS2 and GQDs is not only long range van der Waals interactions, but also chemical interactions involving charge transfer from metal 3d orbital to C 2p orbital of GQDs are also present. Due to this synergic effect, there is enhancement in electronic states near Fermi level which may improve the conductivity of the hybrid structure. Enhanced quantum capacitance, improved conductivity and increased surface area for the hybrid structure may attribute towards superior charge storage performance for the hybrid structures which support experimental data. It is expected that the fabricated, unique, 3D flower NCMS-G-2.5 has potential prospects for developing advanced and high-performance energy storage devices.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transition metal sulfides (TMS) are promising electrode materials for supercapacitor applications due to their unmatchable advantages. To overcome the deficiency of individual metal sulfides, the combination of one or more pseudocapacitive transition metals with conductive carbon material can be a plausible solution. In this work, a simple and one-step hydrothermal synthesis method is used to fabricate the flower-like Ni3S2/CoS2/MoS2 (NCMS) and Ni3S2/CoS2/MoS2-GQDs (NCMS-G) directly on Ni foam and used it as a binder-free electrode for high-performance supercapacitor applications. The NCMS-G-2.5 electrode possessed colossal specific capacitance (2364 Fg−1 at the current density of 2 Ag−1), excellent rate capability (55 % at the current density of 10 Ag−1), as well as good cycling stability (93 % retention after 10,000 cycles at the current density of 20 Ag−1). Furthermore, assembled NCMS-G-2.5//rGO asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device delivered an excellent energy density of 84.8 Wh kg−1 and superior cycling performance (98 % capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles). We have investigated the interactions between Ni3S2/CoS2/MoS2 and carbon based nanostructure GQDs employing state-of-the-art Density Functional Theory (DFT) simulations. The interactions between Ni3S2/CoS2/MoS2 and GQDs is not only long range van der Waals interactions, but also chemical interactions involving charge transfer from metal 3d orbital to C 2p orbital of GQDs are also present. Due to this synergic effect, there is enhancement in electronic states near Fermi level which may improve the conductivity of the hybrid structure. Enhanced quantum capacitance, improved conductivity and increased surface area for the hybrid structure may attribute towards superior charge storage performance for the hybrid structures which support experimental data. It is expected that the fabricated, unique, 3D flower NCMS-G-2.5 has potential prospects for developing advanced and high-performance energy storage devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Serious environmental issues and depletion of fossil energy have led to an increase in the demand for energy storage devices from the past few decades [1,2]. Due to their superior cyclic stability, high power density, and fast charge-discharge mechanism, supercapacitors have gained a lot of attention in the field of energy storage devices. Still, their poor energy density hinders them for broader applications [3,4]. The intended energy density of supercapacitors is E = 0.5 CV2, and it can be achieved by enhancing the potential window (V) and specific capacitance (C) of the system. Both C and V depend on material characteristics such as morphology, crystal structure, porosity, and particle size [5,6]. Broadly supercapacitors are classified into two categories, electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLC), where charge storage happens on the electrode surface, and faradic/pseudocapacitive capacitors, where charge storage defines by surface redox reactions, respectively. Generally, Carbon-based materials come under the EDLC category with specific capacitances in ~170–200 Fg−1 because of their higher specific surface area and porosity [7–10].

Transition metal sulfides (TMS) recently gained more attention than transition metal oxides due to their richer redox properties, lower electronegativity, superior electrical conductivity, higher theoretical specific capacitance, and electrocatalytic activities [11–16]. Metal sulfides such as MoS2 [17], CoS2 [18], and Ni3S2 [19] with various morphologies exhibit admirable electrochemical performance and excellent electrical conductivity. As mentioned earlier, the sulfides capacitive capability can be increased by a simple multi-component synergistic route which can facilitate the transfer of electrons and ions by boosting the electrical conductivity [20–27]. Few literature reports are available of trimetallic Ni-Co-Mo-X (X = oxygen, sulfur, nitrogen, or phosphorus) for supercapacitor applications such as Ni–Zn–Co oxide nanowire arrays [28], Ni-Co–Mo–P materials on a carbon cloth [29], Ni-Co–Mn-based sulfide Nanosheets [30] but there's a dire need for the development of Nickel-Cobalt-Molybdenum sulfides for supercapacitor applications. The previous studies indicated that the ternary metal sulfide electrodes displayed outstanding specific capacitance but suffered poor cycling stability [31–33]. So, a novel class of materials emerges by combining the carbon-based materials with transition metal sulfides to achieve better electrochemical properties and cyclic stability than single carbon materials and metal sulfides.

Among carbon-based materials (graphene, CNTs, porous carbon, etc.) Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) attained considerable attention in the fields of sensors [34,35], photocatalysis [36], drug delivery [37,38], and energy storage and conversion because of their unique physicochemical properties and ultra-small sizes. Typically, GQDs are monolayers of carbon materials with Sp2 hybridization. GQDs possess variable surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl, contributing to the ion−/electron-transfer process due to their robust affinities and abundant electrochemical active sites. Moreover, GQDs have higher specific surface areas, thus providing better contact between active material and electrolyte, increasing the electrochemical behaviour of the prepared material. Here are some recent literatures on transition metal sulfide with GQDs and used for supercapacitor applications [39–42].

Till now, very few reported works available for both the experimental and theoretical investigations (electronic structure, quantum capacitance, orbitals interaction in hybrid, and possible charge transportation) on the metal chalcogenides/carbon allotrope-based hybrids for supercapacitor application. Recently, Samal et al. [43] reported the energy storage performance of MnSe2/MWCNT hybrid to supercapacitor application with DFT examination. The author observed that MnSe2/MWCNT exhibited a higher capacity of 227 Fg−1 at the current density of 1 Ag−1, which is 3.7 times higher than bare MnSe2. The DFT observation also supports in such a way that the computed quantum capacitance is maximum for MnSe2 (200)/CNT compared to bare MnSe2. It is claimed that the CNT increases the energy states around the Fermi level in the MnSe2 (200)/CNT hybrid (from the density of states (D(E)) which indicates the electronic conductivity enhancement in the hybrid structure results in superior electrochemical performance. In another report, Sharma et al. [44] studied the supercapacitive behaviour of VS4//MXene device, which demonstrated an areal capacitance of about 70.9 mF cm−2 at 5 mVs−1 (scan rate). The estimated capacitance value for VS4//MXene is 4.5 times and 6 times higher than the pristine VS4 and MXene (symmetric devices), respectively. In the DFT aspect, the author computed the partial density of states (PDOS) for each element of VS4 (V 3d, S 3p) and Ti3C2 (Ti 3d, C 2p) systems to explore the energy storage mechanism. The valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) of VS4 are dominated by the S 3p and V 3d, respectively. The empty states in the VB of V 3d and CB of S 3p favour for the electrochemical redox behaviour, whereas in Ti3C2, Ti 3d of both VB and CB participated in the charge storage. In addition, the author found the diffusion energy barrier of electrolytic ion (K+) with VS4 (ΔE = 0.17 eV), which is lower than the Ti3C2 (ΔE = 0.28 eV). It was reported that VSe2/MWCNT based electrode exhibited excellent capacitance (233.33 Fg−1 at 2 Ag−1) than those of VSe2 and MWCNT, according to Sree Raj et al. [45] the author carried out PDOS calculation through DFT, for VSe2 (V3d, Se 3p) and SWCNT (C 2p) system to discover the possible charge transportation between them. The results showed that the charge had been transferred from V 3d to C 2p orbital, which improved the conductivity of the hybrid structure. Samal and his group [46] further investigated supercapacitor performance of the ternary hybrid structure of CoSe2/MWCNTs/rGO, which showed a high energy density value of 20.25 Whkg−1 at the current density of 0.3 Ag−1 compared to CoSe2/MWCNTs (13.46 Whkg−1), CoSe2/rGO (14.88 Whkg−1) and pristine CoSe2 (5.59 Whkg−1). From the DFT investigation, the author revealed that the C 2p orbital of MWCNT/rGO was gained charges from the Co 3d orbital of CoSe2, which is confirmed from the energy states enhancement (C 2p) and decrement (Co 3d) at the Fermi level (PDOS). It was concluded that the charge transference was higher in the hybrid system than in other systems, which benefited the enhanced charge storage.

Hence, efforts are being made to fully utilize the active electrode material by developing novel nano architectures to enhance the energy density of the supercapacitors for practical applications. To improve the electrochemical activity and stability of the transition metal sulfides, in this manuscript, we have fabricated a novel Ni3S2/CoS2/MoS2-GQDs (NCMS-G-2.5) composite directly on Ni foam using a facile single-step hydrothermal synthesis method and tested it for high-performance supercapacitor applications. The fabricated NCMS-G-2.5 electrode delivered an ultra-high specific capacitance of 2364 Fg−1 at the current density of 2 Ag−1 with excellent cyclic stability of 93 % after 10,000 cycles. Moreover, the fabricated asymmetric supercapacitor device exhibits an impressive energy density of 84.8 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 568 W kg−1. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to report the effect of GQD inclusion on NCMS for supercapacitor applications in a binder-free approach. We hope that this works paves the way for developing tri metallic materials for high-performance supercapacitors for practical applications.

Analytical grade Ammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O), Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), Urea (CH4N2O), thiourea, citric acid, ethanol, hydrochloric acid was purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used directly without further purification. Nickel foam is purchased from MTI corp., USA. Throughout all our experiments, Deionized water is used.

According to our previous study, the Graphene Quantum Dots were synthesized. Firstly, citric acid (420 mg) and urea (360 mg) were added to 10 ml of DI water and stirred for 45 min to obtain a homogeneous mixture. Then the precursor solution was transferred to a 50 ml autoclave, and hydrothermal synthesis was conducted at 160 °C for 4 h. Later, the black precipitate was collected by centrifugation and vacuum drying at 80 °C for 12 h [53].

The NCMS and NCMS-G electrodes were fabricated by a simple, cost-effective one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. To remove the unwanted impurities and oxidized layer from the Ni foam, it was pre-treated in 3 M HCL and ultra-sonicated for 15 min, then cleaned thoroughly using DI water and ethanol, later vacuum dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Firstly, the precursor solution was prepared by dissolution of 3 mmol of Ammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate, 3 mmol of Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate, 3 mmol of Nickel nitrate hexahydrate, 12 mmol of thiourea, 5 mmol of urea, and a certain of GQDs in 80 ml of DI water and stirred for 30 min in room temperature. Subsequently, the precursor solution is transferred to 100 ml of an autoclave with a rectangular Ni foam (6 cm*1 cm) placed inside and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 16 h. After the autoclave cooled down to room temperature, we have obtained the electrodes washed with DI water and ethanol five times, later vacuum dried at 80 °C overnight. To prepare the NCMS electrode, we have followed the same synthesis procedure except for the addition of GQDs during the preparation of the precursor solution. The mass loading of active materials on Ni foam of NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 is 1.8 and 2.4 mg cm−2, respectively.

To study the effect of GQDs on the electrochemical performance of NCMS electrodes, the amount of GQDs loading (1, 2.5, 5, and 10 mg) during the hydrothermal synthesis is varied and labelled as NCMS-G-x, where x represents the amount of GQDs in milligrams.

The crystal structure of the prepared electrodes was conducted by X-ray diffraction (XRD) in the 2θ range of 10–80° (PANalytical, Cu-Kα radiation with λ = 1.5406 Å). Tensor 37 (Bruker, USA) was used to perform the Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) analysis. The morphologies of the synthesized materials were analyzed via Scanning electron microscopy (SEM; ZEISS ULTRA FEG55). The TEM, HR-TEM, and SAED patterns were obtained on a JEOL JEM 2100 system. RAMAN analysis of the materials conducted using 532 nm laser on a WITec alpha300R system.

Electrochemical measurements of the prepared electrodes were conducted by a three-electrode configuration setup using Autolab 302 N potentiostat under ambient conditions (25 °C) in a 3 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. The fabricated NCMS-G-2.5 electrode, platinum foil (1*1 cm2), and Ag/AgCl (KCl solution) were used as working, counter, and reference electrodes. To analyze the electrochemical performance, Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Galvanostatic Charge – Discharge (GCD) tests were conducted in the range of 5–200 mVs−1 and 1 to 20 Ag−1, respectively. Repetitive GCD cycles (10,000) were used to estimate the cyclic stability of the prepared material at a current density of 20 Ag−1. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted at open circuit potential with a 5 mV amplitude in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz.

The mass-specific capacitance (Cm) from the GCD curves using the equation as follows:

where Cm is mass specific capacitance (Fg−1), I is current density (Ag−1), t is discharge time (s), m is mass of the active material (g) and ΔV is potential window (V), respectively.

Later, an Asymmetric Supercapacitor device (ASC) is fabricated using reduced Graphene Oxide (rGO) as negative electrode and NCMS-G-2.5 electrode as positive electrode. To get the better electrochemical performance from the prepared ASC device it is crucial to adjust the mass balance of the positive and negative electrodes. By using the charge mass balance equation (q+ = q−), we estimated the mass ratio of rGO and NCMS-G-2.5 electrode.

where E is specific energy (Wh kg−1), Cm is mass specific capacitance (Fg−1), P is specific power (W kg−1), ΔV (V) is cell voltage and t (s) is discharge time.

Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) with generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) scheme was employed to perform the DFT calculations. The projector augmented wave (PAW) method was adopted to describe ion-electron interactions [47–50]. The cut-off energy of 500 eV was taken to define the plane-wave basis set. For the geometry optimization, energy and force convergence criteria for each atom were set to 10−5 eV and 0.01 eV/Å, respectively. We took Monkhorst-pack [51] k-point grids of 5 × 3 × 1, 5 × 5 × 1, and 5 × 5 × 1 for Ni3S2/GQD, CoS2/GQD, and 2H-MoS2/GQD hybrid relaxation, respectively. Grimme's DFT-D2 dispersion scheme [52] was used to describe the existence of van der Waals forces between metal chalcogenide and GQD in the hybrid structures.

Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation method of GQDs, NCMS, and NCMS-G-2.5 electrode materials. Firstly, GQDs are prepared from urea and citric acid according to our previous procedure [53] using hydrothermal synthesis method at 160 °C for 4 h (Fig. 1a). Later, the black precipitate was collected by centrifugation and vacuum drying at 80 °C for 12 h. Binder-free NCMS (Fig. 1b) and NCMS-G-2.5 (Fig. 1c) electrodes on Ni foam fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal synthesis technique with and without the addition of GQDs, respectively. Microstructure plays a vital role in the evolution of electrochemical properties of the fabricated material for supercapacitor applications. The 3D flower morphology of binder-free NCMS-G-2.5 electrode on Ni foam provides highly efficient usage of the electroactive material, thus, in turn, resulting in superior electrochemical performance.

Fig. 2 shows the FESEM, TEM, and high-resolution TEM images of NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 electrodes synthesized on Ni foam. The FESEM morphology of NCMS (Fig. 2a) displays the interconnected sheet morphology on the conductive Ni foam. Fig. 2e and Fig. S1 depicts the 3D flower morphology of the NCMS-G-2.5 electrode; from the figure, we can observe the hierarchical flower sizes of the NCMS-G-2.5 electrode. As described in the literature, such hierarchical 3D flower morphology helps in high access of electrolyte ions into the petals, which are pivotal for reducing the interface contact resistance between the electrolyte and electrode [54–57]. Due to the 3D flower morphology, NCMS-G-2.5 shows better electrochemical performance when compared to the NCMS, as described later. TEM is used to evaluate the shape and structure of NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 electrodes further. The TEM images of NCMS (Fig. 2b-c) and NCMS-G-2.5 (Fig. 2f-g) reveal the sheet-like morphology of the electrodes. The high-resolution TEM images of NCMS (Fig. 2d) show the interplanar spacing ~0.28 nm of Ni3S2 (110) crystalline plane and interplanar spacing ~0.61 nm (100) plane of MoS2 phase, which will also be confirmed by the XRD later. However, Fig. 2h exhibit the interplanar spacing around ~0.28 nm, ~0.61 nm, and 0.34 nm of the crystalline plane of Ni3S2 (110), MoS2 (100), and GQDs (002), respectively. The formation of NCMS-G-2.5 composite was confirmed from the TEM results as well [58–60]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of NCMS (Fig. S2a) and NCMS-G-2.5 (Fig. S2b) are labelled as shown in Fig. S2. From the images, it is well known that both the materials exhibited polycrystalline morphology, which is disclosed by well-ordered lattice fringes of Ni3S2 and MoS2, respectively. The corresponding lattice planes MoS2 (100) and Ni3S2 (111) are also presented. The EDAX analysis of the NCMS (Fig. S3) and NCMS-G-2.5 (Fig. S4) shows the elements of Nickel, Cobalt, Molybdenum, respectively. EDAX analysis confirms that no other impurities are presented in the fabricated electrode material and shows the high purity of NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 materials.

The crystal structure of NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 electrodes was analyzed by XRD patterns (Fig. 3 ). The sharp and intense diffracted peaks at 44.6°, 51,9°, and 76.3° corresponds to the (111), (200), and (220) planes of Ni basement. Clearly, the characteristic diffraction peaks at 21.7°, 31.5°, 38.4°, 49.7°, 55.2°, 69.5°, 73.2°, and 78.8° can be well assigned to the (101), (110), (021), (211), (300), (131), (214) and (401) crystal planes of Ni3S2 (JCPDS 44–1418) [53], respectively and in good agreement with the reported literature, indicating the crystallinity of the composite. The diffraction peaks at 39.1° and 48.2° are indexed to the (211) and (220) crystal planes of CoS2 (JCPDS 41–1471) [57], whereas peaks at 27.5° and 30.1° corresponds to the (004) and (100) diffractions planes of MoS2 (JCPDS 06–0097) [59]. The GQDs component was not identified using XRD, attributed to its small proportion and amorphous structure. Using Bragg's law, the interlayer spacing between the highest intensity diffraction peaks of NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 is calculated, and it is to be ∼0.28 nm for Ni3S2 (110) plane and ~ 0.61 nm for MoS2 (100) plane, respectively. From the XRD patterns, we can see the formation of the NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 composites. Moreover, the peak intensities are relatively sharp and intense, which indicates the crystallization of the NCMS-G-2.5 nanosheets. The crystallization is beneficial for increased electrochemical performance and stability of the electrode during long cyclic stability tests. Moreover, there are no extra diffraction peaks obtained in both NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 samples, indicating the purity of the synthesized material.

The Raman spectra of the binder-free NCMS-G-2.5 electrode is displayed in Fig. S5a. The two characteristic peaks which appear at 261 and 470 cm−1 are attributed to the E1 2g and A1g vibrational modes of Molybdenum sulfide (MoS2), respectively. Generally, the A1g mode corresponds to the out of the plane of vibration of Sulfur atoms along with opposite directions. In contrast, the in-plane E1 2g mode is attributed to the vibration of Molybdenum with respect to Oxygen [61]. The Raman peaks at 576 cm−1, 660 cm−1, 767 cm−1, 863 cm−1 and 953 cm−1 are vibrational peaks of Ni3S2 [62]. The addition of GQDs into the NCMS electrode resulted in the appearance of two vibrational peaks at 1360 cm−1, and 1580 cm−1 can be assigned to the D and G bands of carbon [63]. These peaks are an indication of the inclusion of GQDs into the NCMS matrix. Thus, the formation of NCMS-G-2.5 was confirmed by Raman analysis.

To further characterize the NCMS-G-2.5 heterostructure, FT-IR was performed to identify the spectra, as shown in Fig. S5b. The vibrational peaks observed at (C-OH) 1387 cm−1, aromatic carbons (1600 cm−1), CC (1700 cm−1), CH (3170 cm−1), and C-OH (3431 cm−1) correspond to GQDs, which is similar to our previous work [53]. In the FTIR spectra, the vibrational peaks at 770 cm−1 and 606 cm−1 are attributed to Ni3S2 [64]. This study confirms the inclusion of GQDs into the matrix of metal sulfides which was confirmed earlier by XRD, TEM, and Raman analyses. Hence, the obtained results above confirmed the formation of NCMS-G-2.5 by introducing GQDs into the NCMS matrix.

Cobalt Molybdenum Nickel Sulfides prepared on Nickel foam are evaluated for supercapacitor performance by CV, GCD, EIS, and Cyclic stability tests in the three-electrode configuration set up with 3 M KOH used as an electrolyte. The cyclic voltammogram curves of the NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 composites at 5 mVs−1 in the potential range of 0 to 0.55 V are shown in Fig. 4a. The NCMS-G-2.5 composite displays a larger cycle curve area than the NCMS composite, revealing the better electrochemical properties of the NCMS-G-2.5 composite. The NCMS-G-2.5 composite has a lower separation voltage of 0.15 V between oxidation and reduction peaks when compared to NCMS composite 0.24 V indicates the excellent performance of the GQDs doped composite. Obviously, the comparative analysis of GCD curves (Fig. 4b) of NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 at 2 Ag−1 is superior to that of other electrode materials. This is due to the 3D flower morphology of the.

NCMS-G-2.5 composite, which results in the cooperative effect of fast ion transport channels and GQDs with abundant redox-active sites [41]. The cyclic voltammogram curves of NCMS-G-2.5 composite at various sweep rates of 5,10, 25, 50, 100, and 200 mVs−1 shown in Fig. 4c, exhibits the symmetrical anodic and cathodic peaks indicating the pseudocapacitive nature of composite. The current response increases with an increase in the scan rate showing effective charge transport and good rate capability. The following faradic reversible reactions [5–8] proceed as follows: The reactions involve the cations of Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+ mediated by the OH- ions in the alkaline electrolyte attributed to the quasi-reversible electron transfer process contributes to the superior energy storage of NCMS-G-2.5 composite [21,54,60]. Furthermore, the cyclic voltammogram profiles of all electrodes at various sweep rates as shown in Fig. S6 reveals the good reversibility of the electrode.

The galvanostatic charge/discharge of the GQDs induced NCMS-G-2.5 electrode in the voltage range of 0–0.45 V is shown in Fig. 4d. Interestingly, all the GCD curves demonstrated a typical pseudocapacitive nature of the electrode. The charge/discharge performance of the NCMS-G-2.5 was explored at various current densities ranging from 1 to 10 Ag−1. Eq. (1) calculates the specific capacitance from the GCD plots is as high as 2364 and 1066.6 Fg−1 at the current densities of 2 and 10 Ag−1, respectively. When compared to the other electrodes these values are much higher, confirming the collaborative benefit of NCMS and GQDs (Fig. 4e). The GCD curves of the individual metal sulfides (Ni3S2-GQD, CoS2-GQD and MoS2-GQD) are displayed Fig. S7 and from that we can prove that the performance of three pseudocapacitive transition metals is better than that of the single one with GQDs inclusion. Fig. S8 shows the GCD curves of NCMS and NCMS-G-X (1,5,10), where the curves show the pseudocapacitive behaviour same as NCMS-G-2.5. The calculated specific capacitance values of NCMS and NCMS-G-X (1,5,10) are displayed in Fig. S9 where inclusion of GQDs into the matrix of NCMS resulted in an increase in the specific capacitance values. It can be attributed to the inclusion of GQDs providing a conductive pathway for the electrons inside the NCMS matrix. Fig. S10 reveals the specific capacitance of NCMS-G-2.5 by integrating the area portions of the CV plots collected at different scan rates as a function of mass loading.

Fig. 4f shows the cyclic stability of NCMS-G-2.5 electrode material up to 10,000 charge/discharge cycles at a high current density of 20 Ag−1. The electrode material shows a columbic efficiency of 94.7 % with excellent capacity retention of 93 % even after 10,000 cycles, while the NCMS electrode displays the columbic efficiency of 92.8 % after 10,000 cycles (Fig. S11) demonstrated that inclusion of GQDs provided better electrochemical properties. Table 1 (ESI) summarizes the recent literature on sulfide and oxides of various compounds reported. Compared with the all-relevant reports, to the best of our knowledge, the prepared NCMS-G-2.5 electrode exhibits better specific capacitance values with profound columbic efficiency than the others. To evaluate the electrochemical performance of the prepared composite, Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) plays a vital role. EIS measurements were performed (frequency range from 100mHz to 100 kHz) to understand the charge transfer and capacitive behaviour of the electrodes. The Nyquist plots (NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5) and the equivalent circuit are shown in Fig. S12. The admirable charge transfer process in the electrode materials is confirmed by the absence of a semicircle in the high-frequency region shown in the inset of Fig. S12a. To determine the ionic conductivity of the electrodes, bulk solution resistance (RS) values are examined. Evidently, NCMS-G-2.5 electrode materials have a lower diffusion (RW) and solution resistance (RS) than the NCMS electrode material, which signifies that the NCMS-G-2.5 electrode has a rapid ion and electron transport. The combined effect of GQDs inside the matrix of NCMS related to the superior specific capacitance and columbic efficiency values of NCMS-G-2.5 electrode which are discussed as follows. The unique 3D flower morphology of NCMS-G-2.5 electrode results in the more actives electrochemical active sites which elevates the contact between electrode/electrolyte and ultimately results in the maximum harvesting of the specific capacitance. Firstly, the growth of NCMS-G-2.5 composite on 3D Ni foam increases the electrode and electrolyte contact area, which results in swift redox reaction and ion exchange processes. Secondly, the inclusion of high surface area GQDs into the NCMS matrix not only resulted in the promotion of electrons in the microstructure but also showed high exposure to the active sites, which significantly nurtured the electrochemical properties and structural stability of NCMS-G-2.5 composite electrode during long cyclic stability tests [40,53]. Moreover, GQDs produce significant interfacial regions with NCMS electrode and electrolytes due to their small size of only a few nanometres, which allows them to carry several organic groups for surface wettability. Most importantly, GQDs which are the fragments of graphene have their edge effects helps in prevents the agglomeration of the nanosheets of NCMS-G-2.5 electrode during electrochemical tests and helps in stabilization of the structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pseudocapacitance contribution analysis of NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5
ce:section.ce:para:
The obvious oxidation/reduction peaks in the cyclic voltammograms of NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 confirm the pseudocapacitance nature of the electrodes. The overall electrochemical charge storage in the electrode is a combination of both surface capacity and diffusion-controlled processes. Using the power law, we have calculated the superior charge storage mechanisms of the electrode as follows [65,66]:

In the above equation, v is the scan rate (mVs−1), a and b are variable parameters, and ip is the peak current (A). The equation can be rewritten as follows by applying the logarithm on both sides:

From the above we can conclude that b value can be changed from 0.5 to 1. In particular, if a value of b = 1 indicates the superior surface capacity-controlled charge storage mechanism. Meanwhile, a value of b = 0.5 represents the superior diffusion-controlled mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5 (a and c) a linear graph was constructed between v1/2 and I/v1/2 for the reduction peak. The b values of 0.517 and 0.764 (0.5 < b < 1.0) correspond to the NCMS and NCMS-G-2.5 electrode, respectively. These results indicate the semi-infinite diffusion reaction of NCMS-G-2.5 electrode is more controllable than NCMS.

To determine the percentage ratios of surface capacity-controlled and diffusion-controlled processes, the above equation can be modified as follows [67,68]:

From the fitted linear curve of Fig. 5 k1 and k2 values can be calculated. Fig. 5b and d shows the current response from diffusion controlled faradic contribution for the NCMS-G-2.5 and NCMS electrode at 25 mVs−1. The charge from diffusion-controlled faradic contribution of the NCMS-G-2.5 electrode occupied about 31 % when compared to NCMS electrode (20 %) indicates the dominance of diffusion-controlled faradic charge storage mechanism than the surface-controlled charge storage mechanism, which was benefit for long cyclic stability and rapid charge storage [69,70].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of NCMS-G-2.5//rGO asymmetric supercapacitor device
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the superior performance of the NCMS-G-2.5 electrode for practical applications, an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device is fabricated using NCMS-G-2.5 as cathode and reduced Graphene Oxide (rGO) as an anode in 3 M KOH aqueous electrolyte (Fig. 6a). The cyclic voltammogram curves of NCMS-G-2.5 and rGO at 25 mVs−1 are compared in Fig. 6b. From the curves, we can observe that anode (rGO) has a potential window of −1 to 0 V, and cathode (NCMS-G-2.5) has a potential window of 0 to 0.55 V in electrolyte media. The fabricated ASC device has a large operating potential window of 0 to 1.5 V to measure the electrochemical properties in the two-electrode system.

The cyclic voltammogram curves of the NCMS-G-2.5//rGO ASC device at different sweep rates are presented in Fig. 6c. From the shape of curves, we can deduce the hybrid type behaviour of the ASC device, and at higher sweep rates of 50 mVs−1 curves maintaining its original shape confirms the excellent reversibility of the ASC device. GCD measurements were conducted at different current densities (1–5 Ag−1) in the constant potential window of 1–1.5 V, as shown in Fig. 6d. The ASC device exhibited the specific capacitance of 120 Fg−1 at 1 Ag−1 and retains its specific capacitance of 75 Fg−1 at a higher current density of 5 Ag−1 shows the excellent ability of the device. The cyclic stability of the NCMS-G-2.5//rGO device is performed at a current density of 2 Ag−1 for 10,000 cycles. Surprisingly, the ASC device shows excellent columbic efficiency and capacity retention values of 99 % and 98 %, respectively (Fig. 6e).

Energy density and Power density values for the NCMS-G-2.5//rGO were calculated by using Eqs. (3) and (4). From the Ragone plot (Fig. 6f), the ASC device delivered an Energy density of 84.85 Wh Kg−1 at a Power density of 568.8 W Kg−1, and the device-maintained Energy density of 40 Wh Kg−1 even though at a high-Power density of 1178.8 W Kg−1. When compared to those previously reported supercapacitor devices, our NCMS-G-2.5//rGO ASC device shows better specific capacitance, Energy density, and Power density values. MNS-G-2.5//rGO (energy density of 38.9 Wh kg−1 @ 416.6 W kg−1 power density) [53], NCO-0.5 M//JC HASC (energy density of 28.6 Wh kg−1 @ 7.5 kW kg−1 power density) [70], MoS2/C (3:1)//C (energy density of 40 Wh kg-1 @ 200 W kg-1 power density) [71], CoMoS4//rGO (energy density of 27.2 Wh kg−1 @ 400 W kg−1 power density) [72], rGO@CoNi2S4@NiCo LDH//AC (energy density of 57.4 Wh kg−1 @ 323 W kg−1 power density) [73], C@NiMn-OH-Ni3S2/Ni//AC HSC (energy density of 45.3 Wh kg−1 @ 1275 W kg−1 power density) [74], Thus, to fabricate high performance supercapacitor devices addition of GQDs into the matrix of mixed metal sulfides can be a versatile and scalable strategy.

The DFT relaxed structures of Ni3S2 (bulk), CoS2 (bulk), and 2H-MoS2 (monolayer) were disclosed in Fig. 7a-c. The optimized Ni3S2 (Fig. 7a) possesses the lattice constant value of a = 4.08 Å, which is in good agreement with the works of literature of both computational (a = 4.09 Å) [75] and experimental (a = 4.07 Å) [76]. In addition, the bond length of Ni-S (2.28 Å) and Ni-Ni (2.54 Å) reasonably match up with the experimental reported work (Ni-S = 2.29 Å; Ni-Ni = 2.53 Å) [75]. The computed lattice parameter (a = b = c = 5.51 Å) for CoS2 (Fig. 7b) also closely matches with the computationally (a = b = c = 5.38 Å) [77] as well as experimentally (a = b = c = 5.53 Å) [78] obtained values by some other research groups. For the case of MoS2 (Fig. 7c) also, the assessed lattice constant (a = 3.19 Å) and the bond length (Mo-S = 2.40 Å) from DFT are well in accordance with the reported work (a = b = 3.19 Å; Mo-S = 2.42 Å) [79,80]. The reasonable matching lattice parameters and the bond length of optimized structures with available literature give assurance to carry out the further calculation in the right direction. We have computed the total density of states (TDOS) for bulk Ni3S2, bulk CoS2, and 2H-MoS2 (monolayer), which are displayed in Fig. 8a-c. Ni3S2 (Fig. 8a) and CoS2 (Fig. 8b) demonstrate metallic behaviour, which is consistent with the previous literatures [81,82]. But, MoS2 has a semiconducting signature with a bandgap of 1.48 eV (Fig. 8c), which also nearly concur with the reported work [82]. In order to construct a hybrid structure along with graphene quantum dot, we have generated the Ni3S2 (110) and CoS2 (211) slabs from their respective bulk system, which were selected based on the prominent XRD peak of Ni3S2 and CoS2 (XRD patterns). These surface structures were relaxed well and are displayed in Fig. S13a-b. To maintain the periodic boundary condition, we have taken graphene layer instead of graphene quantum dot while making hybrid system, and the relaxed structure also disclosed in Fig. S13c, for our convenience, it is mentioned as GQD. Further, the relaxed GQD layer was stacked on the optimized Ni3S2 (110), CoS2 (211), and 2H-MoS2 structures distinctly at a distance of about 3.5 Å, in the c-axis of the supercell. After the structural optimization, the hybrid of Ni3S2 (110)/GQD, CoS2 (211)/GQD, and MoS2/GQD have an interlayer distance of about 3.3, 3.2, and 3.2 Å, respectively, which are presented in the Fig. 9 a-c. The measured bond length of Ni-S (2.31 Å), Co-S (2.25 Å) and Mo-S (2.56 Å) for the hybrid system gets increased when it is compared with pristine structures (Ni-S (2.16 Å), Co-S (2.21 Å) and Mo-S (2.41 Å)) which claiming that the surface area of the hybrid system was amplified due to the incorporation of carbon nanostructure. Followed by, TDOS were computed for both bare and hybrid cases (Fig. 10 a-c). We can see enhancement in electronic states near Fermi level for the hybrid structure. This signifies that after incorporating GQD with metal sulfide surface, the conductivity of the hybrid gets improved due to interactions between them. This conductivity enhancement favours for the excellent energy storage behaviour of the hybrid systems.

In the extension, we have evaluated the quantum capacitance for bare and hybrid systems using their density of states (DOS) per eV through the following equations [83] (13–15):

where kB indicates Boltzmann's constant and φG specifies the potential window between the electrodes, which is associated with the electrochemical measurement. Fortran Code was established to calculate quantum capacitance employing Eqs. (13) and (14). The total capacitance [84] is associated with the combination of quantum capacitance with electric double-layer capacitance, which is described as follows,
(15)

where CQ stands for quantum capacitance that is the dominant term in the low dimensional system, and CEDL relates the electric double-layer capacitance, which is associated with electrode-electrolyte interactions.

The quantum capacitance versus electrode potential plots was shown in Fig. 11 a-c. In all cases, the hybrid has higher capacitance than the pristine one (Ni3S2 < Ni3S2/GQD, CoS2 < CoS2/GQD, MoS2 < MoS2/GQD), which qualitatively corroborates with the experimental capacitance trend (NCMS<NCMS@G). Moreover, we have plotted the partial density of states (PDOS) for both bare and hybrid systems to reveal the possible charge transference between the orbitals in the hybrid structures. Fig. 12 a-c compares PDOS for Ni 3d orbital of Ni3S2, Co 3d orbital of CoS2, and Mo 4d orbital of MoS2 when it is alone (bare) and attached with the GQD (hybrid). From the PDOS, Ni 3d, Co 3d and Mo 4d (Fig. 12 a-c; lower panel) of hybrid structures having reduced electronic states around the Fermi level when it is compared to their corresponding bare systems (Fig. 12 a-c; upper panel), which demonstrates that charge has transferred from Ni 3d to C 2p, Co 3d to C 2p and Mo 4d to C 2p. For Ni 3d and Mo 4d orbitals, there are prominent occupied states in the valence band and empty states in the conduction band whereas for Co 3d, both occupied and unoccupied states are less prominent (Fig.12 a-c; upper panel). Presence of occupied and empty electronic states below and above Fermi level may attribute towards Faradic redox reaction as mentioned in the previous section.

To give further support, the PDOS of C 2p are computed for isolated GQD and the hybrid structures as shown in Fig. 12d, here, we can observe that states are enhanced in C 2p of hybrid structures compared to isolated GQD (C 2p), which implies C 2p is gaining charges from the d orbital of Ni, Co, Mo. Similar observations have been perceived in the VSe2/SWCNT [45] and CoSe2/rGO/SWCNT [46] hybrid systems warrant our present findings. From both the PDOS analysis, it is concluded that the orbital interactions and charge transfer may improve the overall conductivity of the hybrid system.

In summary, we have successfully fabricated a 3D flower morphological NCMS-G-2.5 electrode on Ni foam using a single step, facile and cost-effective hydrothermal synthesis method. The addition of GQDs would provide the additional conductive sites and improve the electrical conductivity of the electrode. The NCMS-G-2.5 displays the best electrochemical performance, including a large specific capacitance of 2364 Fg−1 at a current density of 2 Ag−1 and excellent capacitance retention over 93 % (at 20 Ag−1) after 10,000 cycles. In addition, the assembled asymmetric device (NCMS-G-2.5//rGO) shows an energy density of 84.8 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 568 W kg−1 and still retains 40 Wh kg−1 even at a high-power density of 1178.8 W kg−1. The electrochemical performance of the NCMS-G-2.5//rGO ASC device has outperformed those of previously reported ternary metal sulfide-based ASC devices. The amplified electrochemical performance of the hybrid system (NCMS-G-2.5) was rationalized with the support of DFT simulations. The computed TDOS having more populated energy states near the Fermi level which may signify that the hybrid structure having enhanced conductivity. The surplus of energy states is due to the charge transfer from 3d/4d orbital (Ni3S2, CoS2, and MoS2) to C 2p orbital (GQD) in the hybrid structure. The computed quantum capacitance trend is as follows Ni3S2 < Ni3S2/GQD, CoS2 < CoS2/GQD, MoS2 < MoS2/GQD, which agree with the experimental results (NCMS<NCMS-G-2.5). Notably, the electronic properties and quantum capacitance investigation were qualitatively encouraging our obtained experimental findings. In conclusion, the colossal electrochemical performance suggests that the as-fabricated 3D flower NCMS-G-2.5 material could serve as potential electrode materials in practical energy storage applications.
